title: 38 CFR Quick Reference
desc: A veteran-to-veteran lookup guide to the most important sections of 38 CFR that govern your VA disability claim

sections.heading: :book: What Is 38 CFR?
sections.body:
Title 38 of the Code of Federal Regulations (`38 CFR`) is the **rulebook that governs everything** the VA does with your disability claim. It's not a suggestion box -- it's **binding law**.

> [!important] When a rater denies your claim or assigns a rating, they're required to follow 38 CFR. When the Board of Veterans' Appeals reviews your case, they apply 38 CFR. When you cite the specific regulation, it **forces the VA to address it directly**.

The sections you'll deal with most fall into two main parts:

| Part | What It Covers |
|------|---------------|
| **Part 3** (Adjudication) | How claims are processed, what evidence is needed, service connection, effective dates, reopening claims |
| **Part 4** (Rating Schedule) | How conditions are rated, percentage criteria, diagnostic codes for each condition |
| **Parts 19 & 20** | Board of Veterans' Appeals procedures |
| **Part 17** | Medical care and benefits |

> [!check] Knowing even a handful of these sections gives you a major advantage. This guide covers the sections that matter most -- the ones that come up in nearly every claim and appeal.

> [!tip] When writing your statement or appeal, cite the **specific CFR section number**. It shows the rater you know the rules and forces them to explain why they didn't follow them.

sections.heading: :shield: Service Connection Basics
sections.body:
These are the regulations that determine whether the VA recognizes your condition as connected to your military service. **Know these cold.**

---

### `38 CFR 3.303` -- Principles of Service Connection (Direct)

**What it says:** A disability is service-connected if it was incurred in or aggravated by active military service. You need a **current diagnosis**, an **in-service event or injury**, and a **medical nexus** linking the two.

> [!check] Every direct service connection claim starts here. If the VA denies you for "no nexus," cite `3.303(a)` and get a nexus letter.

---

### `38 CFR 3.303(b)` -- Continuity of Symptomatology

**What it says:** For chronic diseases listed in `3.309(a)`, you can establish service connection by showing **continuous symptoms from service to the present** -- even without a nexus opinion.

> [!tip] If you have a chronic condition (like arthritis or hearing loss) that started in service and never went away, but you lack a formal nexus letter, this is your path. Document the continuous symptom timeline.

---

### `38 CFR 3.304(f)` -- Service Connection for PTSD

**What it says:** PTSD requires a **current diagnosis**, **credible supporting evidence** that the stressor occurred, and a **link** between the stressor and the diagnosis.

> [!important] For **combat veterans**, the stressor is **presumed** if consistent with the circumstances of service. Cite `3.304(f)(2)` -- the VA can't demand specific stressor documentation if your stressor is consistent with combat service.

---

### `38 CFR 3.309` -- Presumptive Conditions

**What it says:** Certain chronic diseases are **presumed service-connected** if they manifest to a degree of 10% or more within **one year** of separation.

**Common presumptive conditions include:**
- Arthritis
- Sensorineural hearing loss
- Tinnitus
- Diabetes (for herbicide-exposed veterans)
- Many others

> [!warning] Check the specific lists in `3.309(a)`, `(b)`, and `(e)`. Each subsection covers different exposure types (chronic diseases, herbicide agents, radiation).

---

### `38 CFR 3.310` -- Secondary Service Connection

**What it says:** A disability that is **proximately due to** or **aggravated by** a service-connected condition is itself service-connected. This includes conditions **caused by** or **permanently worsened** by an already-connected disability.

> [!tip] Common examples: service-connected knee injury causes hip problems, service-connected PTSD leads to sleep apnea. Don't forget the **aggravation** theory -- you don't need to prove causation, just worsening.

---

### `38 CFR 3.317` -- Gulf War Presumptives

**What it says:** Veterans who served in the **Southwest Asia theater** can get service connection for **undiagnosed illnesses** and **medically unexplained chronic multisymptom illnesses** that manifest to 10% or more.

> [!important] This covers conditions like chronic fatigue syndrome, fibromyalgia, and functional gastrointestinal disorders. If you served in Iraq, Afghanistan, or other Southwest Asia locations and have chronic symptoms that doctors can't fully explain, this regulation is built for you.

sections.heading: :search: Evidence and Claims Development
sections.body:
These sections control what evidence the VA **must** help you get, and how they handle what you submit. The VA has obligations here -- hold them to it.

---

### `38 CFR 3.159` -- VA's Duty to Assist

**What it says:** The VA is **legally required** to help you develop your claim. That means:

- Getting your **service records**
- Getting your **VA treatment records**
- Getting relevant **private medical records** you identify
- Providing a **medical examination** when one is needed
- **Notifying you** of what evidence is needed and who is responsible for getting it

> [!check] Cite `3.159(c)` for their duty to obtain records and `3.159(d)` for when an examination is required. If the VA denies your claim without providing a C&P exam or without getting records you identified, that's a duty-to-assist failure.

---

### `38 CFR 3.103` -- Due Process Rights

**What it says:** You have the right to:
- A **hearing**
- **Representation**
- **Present evidence and argument**

The VA must consider **all evidence of record** and provide **adequate reasons and bases** for any decision.

> [!warning] If the VA issues a decision that ignores evidence you submitted, that's a due process violation under `3.103`. The decision must address your evidence -- they can't just pretend it doesn't exist.

---

### `38 CFR 3.156` -- New and Material Evidence

**What it says:** A previously denied claim can be **reopened** if you submit **new evidence** (not previously considered) that is **material** (relates to an unestablished fact necessary for the claim).

> [!tip] Under the Appeals Modernization Act, you file a **Supplemental Claim** with new and relevant evidence. You don't need to prove your whole case again -- just submit evidence that addresses the **specific reason** your claim was denied before.

> [!important] Read your prior denial carefully. It tells you what was missing. Get evidence that fills that gap, then file a Supplemental Claim citing `3.156`.

---

### `38 CFR 3.326` -- Examinations

**What it says:** Where VA determines an examination is necessary, one will be provided. Reexaminations are scheduled when improvement is indicated. You have the right to an **adequate examination**.

> [!warning] If your C&P exam was inadequate -- too short, examiner didn't review your file, didn't test range of motion properly -- cite `3.326` alongside **Barr v. Nicholson** and request a new exam.

sections.heading: :chart: Rating Principles
sections.body:
These are the foundational rules that control how the VA assigns your disability percentage. **Know these cold** -- they apply to every single rated condition.

---

### `38 CFR 4.1` -- Essentials of Evaluative Rating

**What it says:** Disability ratings are meant to compensate for the **average impairment of earning capacity** resulting from service-connected conditions. The rating schedule is a guide, not a rigid formula.

> [!tip] Use this when arguing that the VA should consider the **real-world impact** of your disability on your ability to work, not just check-box criteria.

---

### `38 CFR 4.3` -- Resolution of Reasonable Doubt

**What it says:** When the evidence for and against a claim is in **approximate balance**, the benefit of the doubt goes to the veteran.

> [!important] This is your **single most powerful regulation**. Every claim where the evidence is close -- if the medical evidence could support either a higher or lower rating, cite `4.3` and demand the higher one.

---

### `38 CFR 4.7` -- Higher of Two Evaluations

**What it says:** When the symptoms fall **between two rating levels**, and the disability picture more nearly approximates the criteria for the **higher rating**, the higher rating is assigned.

> [!check] When your symptoms don't perfectly match the next higher rating but are clearly worse than the current one, pair `4.7` with `4.3` for a powerful argument. You don't need to check every box -- you need to show the overall picture fits better at the higher level.

---

### `38 CFR 4.10` -- Functional Impairment

**What it says:** The basis of disability evaluations is the ability to **function under ordinary conditions of daily life**, including employment. The rating should reflect **functional impairment**, not just clinical measurements.

> [!warning] If the VA focuses on a single test result (like range of motion) while ignoring how the condition actually affects your daily life and work, cite `4.10`. The regulation says functional impact is what matters.

---

### `38 CFR 4.25` -- Combined Ratings Table

**What it says:** Multiple disability ratings are combined using the **Combined Ratings Table**, not simple addition. Each additional disability is applied to the **remaining efficiency**. The result is rounded to the nearest 10%.

| Example | The Math |
|---------|----------|
| 50% + 30% | Does NOT equal 80% |
| Actual calculation | 50% + 30% of remaining 50% = 65% |
| Final rating | Rounds to **70%** |

> [!important] This is "VA math" and it matters for **TDIU thresholds** and overall compensation. Don't assume your ratings add up the way you'd expect.

---

### `38 CFR 4.6` -- Evaluation of Evidence

**What it says:** The rating specialist must interpret examination reports **in light of the whole recorded history**. A single examination is not sufficient to establish entitlement.

> [!warning] When the VA relies on one bad C&P exam to deny or reduce your rating while ignoring **years of treatment records**, cite `4.6`. They're required to look at the whole picture.

sections.heading: :brain: Mental Health Ratings
sections.body:
Mental health conditions are rated under `38 CFR 4.130` using the **General Rating Formula for Mental Disorders**. This single formula applies to nearly all mental health diagnostic codes.

---

**Rating levels at a glance:**

| Rating | Level of Impairment | Key Indicators |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **0%** | Diagnosed but minimal | Symptoms don't interfere with work/social functioning |
| **10%** | Mild/transient | Decreased work efficiency only during significant stress, or controlled by medication |
| **30%** | Occasional decrease | Intermittent inability to perform tasks; depressed mood, anxiety, chronic sleep impairment, mild memory loss |
| **50%** | Reduced reliability | Panic attacks weekly+, difficulty with complex commands, impaired judgment, difficulty maintaining relationships |
| **70%** | Deficiencies in most areas | Suicidal ideation, near-continuous panic/depression, impaired impulse control, neglect of hygiene, inability to maintain relationships |
| **100%** | Total impairment | Persistent delusions/hallucinations, danger to self/others, can't perform daily activities, disorientation |

> [!important] The symptoms listed at each level are **examples, not requirements**. Under **Mauerhan v. Principi**, the VA can't deny a higher rating just because you don't check every listed symptom. The question is whether your **overall level of impairment** matches the rating level.

**Key diagnostic codes:**

| Code | Condition |
|------|-----------|
| `DC 9411` | PTSD |
| `DC 9434` | Major Depressive Disorder |
| `DC 9400` | Generalized Anxiety Disorder |
| `DC 9432` | Bipolar Disorder |
| `DC 9413` | Adjustment Disorder |

> [!tip] When documenting your symptoms for a mental health claim, focus on **functional impact** -- how your symptoms affect work, relationships, daily tasks, and safety. Don't just list symptoms; describe what you **can't do** because of them.

> [!warning] The jump from 50% to 70% is where most veterans get stuck. The key differentiator is **deficiencies in most areas** -- document how your condition impacts work, family, judgment, thinking, AND mood. You need problems across multiple domains, not just one.

sections.heading: :fire: Musculoskeletal Ratings
sections.body:
If you have joint, back, or muscle conditions, these sections are essential. **The VA frequently underrates musculoskeletal conditions by ignoring functional loss.**

---

### `38 CFR 4.40` -- Functional Loss

**What it says:** Disability of the musculoskeletal system is primarily the inability to perform **normal working movements** with normal excursion, strength, speed, coordination, and endurance. **Functional loss from pain must be rated to the same degree as functional loss from actual anatomical damage.**

> [!important] If the VA rates you based on range of motion alone without considering **pain, fatigue, weakness, or lack of endurance**, they're violating `4.40`. Pain IS functional loss.

---

### `38 CFR 4.45` -- The Joints

**What it says:** When evaluating joints, the VA must consider:
- Pain on movement
- Swelling and deformity
- **Instability**
- Disturbance of locomotion
- Interference with sitting and standing
- Weight-bearing capacity

> [!warning] If the VA only measured your range of motion and ignored instability, swelling, or how the joint actually performs during activities, they didn't follow `4.45`.

---

### `38 CFR 4.59` -- Painful Motion

**What it says:** Joints that are actually painful, unstable, or malaligned due to healed injury are entitled to **at least the minimum compensable rating** for the joint.

> [!check] If the VA rates your painful joint at **0%**, that's wrong. If you have documented painful motion, you are entitled to **at least 10%** under `4.59`. This applies to any joint with painful motion, **even if range of motion is technically normal**.

---

### `38 CFR 4.71a` -- Schedule of Ratings, Musculoskeletal System

This is the rating schedule itself. **Key diagnostic codes:**

| Code | Condition |
|------|-----------|
| `DC 5003` | Degenerative arthritis (rated on X-ray + limitation of motion) |
| `DC 5010` | Traumatic arthritis (rated as degenerative) |
| `DC 5201` | Limitation of motion of the arm |
| `DC 5237` | Lumbosacral strain |
| `DC 5242` | Degenerative arthritis of the spine |
| `DC 5243` | Intervertebral disc syndrome (incapacitating episodes) |
| `DC 5260` | Limitation of flexion of the leg |
| `DC 5261` | Limitation of extension of the leg |
| `DC 5257` | Recurrent subluxation or lateral instability of the knee |

> [!danger] **DeLuca v. Brown reminder:** The VA **must** consider functional loss due to pain, weakened movement, excess fatigability, and incoordination when rating musculoskeletal disabilities. If your C&P examiner didn't test **repetitive use** and note additional functional loss, the exam may be inadequate.

> [!tip] For knee claims, you can potentially get **separate ratings** under `DC 5260` (flexion), `DC 5261` (extension), AND `DC 5257` (instability) for the same knee. These are different manifestations and can be rated separately.

sections.heading: :money: TDIU (Total Disability Based on Individual Unemployability)
sections.body:
TDIU pays you at the **100% rate** even if your combined rating is less than 100%, when your service-connected disabilities prevent you from maintaining **substantially gainful employment**.

---

### `38 CFR 4.16(a)` -- Schedular TDIU

**What it says:** TDIU is available when you meet **either** threshold:

| Threshold | Requirement |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Single disability** | One SC disability rated at **60% or more** |
| **Combined** | Combined rating of **70% or more** with at least one disability at **40% or more** |

...AND your service-connected disabilities **prevent substantially gainful employment**.

> [!check] If you meet the thresholds and can't work due to your service-connected conditions, file **VA Form 21-8940**.

> [!important] **Combination rule:** Disabilities from a **common etiology**, single accident, or affecting a **single body system** are treated as ONE disability for the 60%/40% threshold. So a 30% knee and 20% hip from the same injury combine into a single 50% disability for TDIU purposes.

---

### `38 CFR 4.16(b)` -- Extraschedular TDIU

**What it says:** Veterans who **don't meet the schedular thresholds** but whose SC disabilities still prevent substantially gainful employment can be referred to the **Director of Compensation Service** for extraschedular TDIU consideration.

> [!warning] The evidence bar is higher for extraschedular TDIU. You'll want **strong vocational and medical opinions** that specifically address why your combined disabilities prevent all forms of substantially gainful employment.

---

**What counts as "substantially gainful employment"?**

> [!tip] Substantially gainful employment means **more than marginal employment**. Under `4.16(a)`, marginal employment is defined as earning below the **federal poverty threshold**. Working in a **protected or sheltered environment** (family business, employer making special accommodations) may also be marginal employment on a facts-found basis. If you're only working because your employer makes special exceptions for you, that's not substantially gainful.

sections.heading: :flag: Appeals and Effective Dates
sections.body:
These sections determine **when your benefits start** and **how you challenge bad decisions**. Getting these right can mean thousands in back pay.

---

### `38 CFR 3.400` -- Effective Dates

**What it says:** Generally, the effective date is the **date of receipt of the claim** or the **date entitlement arose**, whichever is **later**.

> [!important] If you filed an **Intent to File** (`38 CFR 3.155`), your effective date goes back to the date the intent was received -- giving you **up to one year** to submit the complete claim. Always file an ITF first.

---

### `38 CFR 3.400(o)(2)` -- Increased Rating Effective Dates

**What it says:** For increased ratings, the effective date is the **earliest date it is factually ascertainable** that an increase occurred, if the claim is received within **one year** of that date.

> [!tip] If you got a higher rating but the VA only made it effective from the date you filed, check your medical records. If they show your condition worsened **before** you filed, cite `3.400(o)(2)` to push the effective date back up to one year earlier.

---

### `38 CFR 3.105` -- Clear and Unmistakable Error (CUE)

**What it says:** Previous VA decisions can be **reversed or amended** if there was clear and unmistakable error -- meaning the correct facts were not before the adjudicator, or the law was **incorrectly applied**, and the outcome would have been **manifestly different** but for the error.

> [!danger] CUE is a **high bar**. It's not a disagreement with how evidence was weighed -- it's an undeniable error in fact or law. But if you can prove it, you can get an effective date going back to the **original wrong decision**, potentially years of back pay.

---

### `38 CFR 20.200` -- Filing an Appeal (AMA)

**What it says:** Under the Appeals Modernization Act, you have **three review lanes** after a decision:

| Lane | What It Is | Best For |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Supplemental Claim** | Submit new evidence | You have new evidence that addresses the denial reason |
| **Higher Level Review** | Same evidence, different reviewer | You think the rater made an error with existing evidence |
| **Board Appeal** | BVA review with optional hearing | You want a Veterans Law Judge to review the case |

> [!check] You have **one year** from the date of the decision to choose your lane. Pick the one that matches your situation.

---

### `38 CFR 20.302` -- Time Limits for Filing

**What it says:** A Notice of Disagreement must be filed within **one year** of the date of the decision. Missing this deadline generally makes the decision **final**.

> [!warning] Track your deadlines religiously. If your one-year window is closing, file an **Intent to File** or **NOD immediately** -- you can gather more evidence later. Missing the deadline is one of the most common and costly mistakes veterans make.

sections.heading: :star: Special Monthly Compensation (SMC)
sections.body:
SMC provides **additional compensation beyond the standard rating schedule** for veterans with severe disabilities or combinations of disabilities. This is money many veterans don't know they're entitled to.

---

### `38 CFR 3.350` -- Special Monthly Compensation Rates

**What it says:** SMC is paid at different levels (designated by letters **K through T**) for specific conditions:

| SMC Level | Triggers |
|-----------|----------|
| **SMC-K** | Loss of use of a creative organ, one foot/hand (most common) |
| **SMC-L** | Need for aid and attendance |
| **SMC-S** | Housebound status |
| **SMC-M through T** | Increasingly severe combinations of losses |

> [!tip] **SMC-K** is commonly awarded alongside conditions like **erectile dysfunction** secondary to PTSD medication or diabetes. If you're on medications that cause these side effects, file for SMC-K.

---

### `38 CFR 3.352` -- Aid and Attendance and Housebound

**Aid and Attendance (SMC-L)** requires showing you need the **regular aid of another person** to perform daily functions:
- Dressing and bathing
- Feeding
- Attending to the wants of nature
- Protecting yourself from the **hazards of daily life**

> [!important] **SMC-S (Housebound) shortcut:** If you have a single disability rated **100%** (schedular or TDIU based on a single disability) PLUS additional SC disabilities independently rated at **60% or more**, you **automatically qualify** for SMC-S. Many veterans are entitled to this but never claim it.

> [!check] **SMC-K stacking:** You can receive **multiple awards** of SMC-K if you have multiple qualifying losses. Loss of use of a creative organ AND hearing loss in one ear = **two SMC-K payments**. Each one adds roughly $120/month.

> [!warning] SMC is often overlooked by both veterans and raters. If you have severe disabilities, check whether any SMC level applies to you. It's additional money on top of your regular compensation.

sections.heading: :tools: How to Use These Sections in Your Claim
sections.body:
Knowing the regulation numbers is only half the battle. Here's how to **actually weaponize them**.

---

**Where to cite CFR sections:**

| Filing Type | How to Use CFR |
|------------|----------------|
| **Statement in Support (VA Form 21-4138)** | Cite the specific section when arguing your point |
| **Supplemental Claim** | Identify what was missing before, cite the reg your new evidence satisfies |
| **Board Appeal** | Cite the regulation the Regional Office failed to apply |
| **Higher Rating Request** | Pair `4.7` (higher of two evaluations) with `4.3` (reasonable doubt) |

> [!quote] Example statement: "Under `38 CFR 4.3`, when the evidence for and against my claim is in approximate balance, the benefit of the doubt must be resolved in my favor. My private physician's opinion supports a 50% rating while the C&P examiner's opinion supports 30%. I respectfully request that reasonable doubt be applied."

> [!quote] Example supplemental claim: "My prior claim was denied for lack of a nexus. I am submitting a medical nexus opinion from Dr. Smith establishing the required link under `38 CFR 3.303(a)`."

---

**Board Appeal targeting:**

| If the RO Did This... | Cite This |
|-----------------------|-----------|
| Ignored painful motion | `4.59` |
| Didn't consider functional loss during flare-ups | `4.40` and `4.45` |
| Relied on one exam, ignored treatment records | `4.6` |
| Didn't provide an exam | `3.159(d)` |
| Ignored your lay evidence | `3.303(a)` |

> [!check] **The power moves:**

- **Always** file an **Intent to File** (`38 CFR 3.155`) before gathering evidence -- it locks in your effective date for up to one year
- **Request copies** of your C-file so you can see exactly what evidence the VA has
- Get **buddy statements** for anything hard to prove with medical records alone
- If your C&P exam was inadequate, **request a new one** and cite the specific regulation violated
- Keep a **symptom log** -- it's powerful evidence when combined with rating criteria from Part 4

> [!warning] Don't just throw regulation numbers at the VA without connecting them to your facts. State the regulation, explain what it requires, and show how your evidence meets that requirement. That's the formula that works.

> [!tip] The most powerful regulation combos: `4.7` + `4.3` for higher ratings, `3.303` + `3.310` for secondary claims, and `3.159` + `3.326` when the VA failed its duty to assist.
